metadata.summary: LlamaIndex IngestionPipeline: document loading, metadata extraction (TitleExtractor, SummaryExtractor), node parsing strategies, and vector store persistence (Chroma, Pinecone).

content.title: LlamaIndex Ingestion Pipeline
content.summary: LlamaIndex IngestionPipeline chains document loaders, metadata extractors, and node parsers into a single reusable pipeline. Connect to a persistent vector store (Chroma or Pinecone) via StorageContext to avoid re-embedding on restart. Tune chunking strategy — SentenceSplitter for speed, SemanticSplitterNodeParser for coherence — based on query patterns.

why.p: Ad-hoc ingestion (load docs → parse → embed inline) works for demos but breaks at scale: no caching, no metadata enrichment, and re-embedding on every restart. IngestionPipeline centralizes transformations and can cache intermediate results, cutting re-ingestion cost for unchanged documents. Persistent vector stores decouple index construction from query serving, which is mandatory for production systems that restart without re-embedding.

Metadata extraction (titles, summaries, keywords) at ingestion time enables filtered retrieval and reduces hallucination: the LLM gets structured context alongside raw text, improving faithfulness scores on multi-hop queries.

list.item: Building a production RAG system that must survive restarts without re-embedding the corpus.
Corpus requires metadata enrichment (auto-extracted titles, summaries, keywords) for filtered retrieval.
Multiple document types from different sources — IngestionPipeline unifies loading, parsing, and embedding in one call.
Long documents where chunk boundary choice materially affects answer quality — compare SentenceSplitter vs SemanticSplitter.
Prototype with fewer than 100 documents — VectorStoreIndex.from_documents() inline is simpler and sufficient.
Fully dynamic data with real-time updates — LlamaIndex indexes are append-friendly but not designed for continuous stream ingestion; use a dedicated streaming pipeline.
Cost is the hard constraint on a small corpus — metadata extractors (TitleExtractor, SummaryExtractor) fire LLM calls per document; disable them if the corpus is large and metadata is not needed.

section.name: document-loading
section.p: LlamaIndex provides SimpleDirectoryReader for files and SimpleWebPageReader for URLs. Custom metadata can be attached at Document construction time or via a metadata extractor in the pipeline.

rule.statement: Always attach source metadata (file path, URL, date) at document creation time. Metadata filters at retrieval time require that metadata keys exist on every node; missing keys cause silent retrieval gaps.
rule.rationale: Metadata is stored per-node in the vector store. If nodes from different ingestion runs have inconsistent keys, metadata filters silently skip nodes lacking the expected key — retrieval appears to work but misses documents.

example.title: SimpleDirectoryReader with metadata
example.code:
from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader
documents = SimpleDirectoryReader( input_dir="./documents", recursive=True, required_exts=[".txt", ".md", ".pdf"] ).load_data()
# Manual metadata on custom documents from llama_index.core import Document
documents = [ Document( text="Document content here", metadata={ "source": "manual", "author": "Jane", "date": "2024-01-01" } ) ]

section.name: ingestion-pipeline-metadata
section.p: IngestionPipeline chains transformations in order. Metadata extractors run LLM calls — budget them explicitly. Cache the pipeline to skip already-embedded documents on re-run.

rule.statement: Restrict IngestionPipeline metadata extractors to the minimum needed: add TitleExtractor only when document titles are absent; add KeywordExtractor only when keyword filtering will be used at retrieval. Each extractor fires one LLM call per document chunk.
rule.rationale: SummaryExtractor on a 10 000-document corpus fires 10 000 LLM calls. At gpt-4o pricing (input) that is material cost for data that may not improve retrieval quality. Profile extraction cost before enabling.

example.title: IngestionPipeline with selective metadata extraction
example.code:
from llama_index.core.extractors import ( TitleExtractor, SummaryExtractor, KeywordExtractor, ) from llama_index.core.ingestion import IngestionPipeline from llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceSplitter
pipeline = IngestionPipeline( transformations=[ SentenceSplitter(chunk_size=1024, chunk_overlap=200), TitleExtractor(), # only if docs lack titles KeywordExtractor(), # only if keyword filtering needed ] )
nodes = pipeline.run(documents=documents)
example.note: SummaryExtractor omitted — adds cost without retrieval benefit in most cases.

section.name: node-parsing-strategies
section.p: The chunking strategy is the single biggest lever for RAG quality. Start with SentenceSplitter; upgrade to SemanticSplitterNodeParser when chunk coherence tests show boundary artifacts.

rule.statement: Start with SentenceSplitter(chunk_size=1024, chunk_overlap=200) as the baseline. Upgrade to SemanticSplitterNodeParser only after measuring that sentence-boundary splits produce incoherent chunks on your corpus. Never start with semantic splitting — it is slower and requires a calibration pass.
rule.rationale: SemanticSplitterNodeParser calls the embedding model once per sentence pair to detect breakpoints. On a 10 000-page corpus this is expensive. SentenceSplitter is deterministic and fast. The 200-token overlap ensures context continuity at boundaries without full document redundancy.

example.title: SentenceSplitter baseline
example.code:
from llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceSplitter
splitter = SentenceSplitter( chunk_size=1024, chunk_overlap=200 ) nodes = splitter.get_nodes_from_documents(documents)

example.title: SemanticSplitterNodeParser for coherent chunks
example.code:
from llama_index.core.node_parser import SemanticSplitterNodeParser from llama_index.core import Settings
semantic_splitter = SemanticSplitterNodeParser( buffer_size=1, breakpoint_percentile_threshold=95, embed_model=Settings.embed_model ) nodes = semantic_splitter.get_nodes_from_documents(documents)
example.note: breakpoint_percentile_threshold=95 means only the top 5% most dissimilar adjacent sentences trigger a split — conservative and suitable for long prose.

antipattern.description: Using chunk_size=256 without increasing chunk_overlap proportionally.
antipattern.reason: At 256 tokens, chunk_overlap=20 (default) covers only ~8% of the chunk — boundary context is nearly absent. At small chunk sizes, set overlap to at least 25% of chunk size.

section.name: vector-store-persistence
section.p: Persistent vector stores decouple ingestion from serving. StorageContext wraps the vector store and is passed to VectorStoreIndex. Load existing indexes without re-embedding using load_index_from_storage or VectorStoreIndex.from_vector_store.

rule.statement: Always persist indexes to a named vector store (Chroma, Pinecone, Qdrant) for any corpus larger than a demo. Tag snapshots with a version identifier (timestamp or content hash) so agents and evaluation runs can load a specific snapshot.
rule.rationale: Without persistence, the index is rebuilt on every restart — re-embedding costs scale with corpus size. Version tagging enables reproducible evaluation: an agent can load the exact index snapshot that was evaluated, not a drifted live index.

example.title: Chroma persistent index
example.code:
import chromadb from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, StorageContext from llama_index.vector_stores.chroma import ChromaVectorStore
chroma_client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db") chroma_collection = chroma_client.get_or_create_collection("documents")
vector_store = ChromaVectorStore(chroma_collection=chroma_collection) storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(vector_store=vector_store)
# First run: build index index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents( documents, storage_context=storage_context )
# Subsequent runs: load without re-embedding index = VectorStoreIndex.from_vector_store(vector_store)

example.title: Pinecone index with metadata filtering
example.code:
from pinecone import Pinecone from llama_index.vector_stores.pinecone import PineconeVectorStore from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, StorageContext
pc = Pinecone(api_key="your-api-key") pinecone_index = pc.Index("your-index")
vector_store = PineconeVectorStore(pinecone_index=pinecone_index) storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(vector_store=vector_store)
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents( documents, storage_context=storage_context )
example.note: Pinecone supports namespace-based partitioning — use namespaces to isolate corpus versions without separate indexes.

example.title: Load persisted index from disk
example.code:
from llama_index.core import load_index_from_storage, StorageContext
storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults( persist_dir="./storage" ) index = load_index_from_storage(storage_context)

checklist.check: Attach source metadata (path, date, author) at Document creation — not as a post-processing step.
Audit IngestionPipeline extractor cost before enabling SummaryExtractor on large corpora.
Start with SentenceSplitter(1024, 200) as baseline; switch to semantic splitting only after measuring boundary quality.
Persist indexes to a named vector store; tag snapshots with version identifiers.
Load existing indexes with from_vector_store() to avoid re-embedding on restart.
